---
title: "Linguistic Complexity Across Institutional and Public-Facing Communication in the U.S. House of Representatives"
author: "Nikolina Djogova"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
editor: visual
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

Political communication plays a central role in shaping how information is conveyed, interpreted, and acted upon. In recent years, increasing attention is being given to how politicians communicate with broader audiences, particularly in context outside formal institutions. However, less attention has been paid to whether communication within institutional settings has also changed over time.

## Relevance

Legislative speech, such as that from the US Congressional record, represents a structured and consistent form of political communication. These speeches are produced within formal constraints and are intended to document legislative activity, yet they also serve as a medium through which political actors present arguments, frame issues, and signal positions.

## Project Overview

This project examines whether there have been measurable changes in the linguistic characteristics of House floor speech over time. In particular, it focuses on whether speech length and complexity show systematic variation across years. Understanding these patterns provides insight into whether broader shifts in political communication are reflected even within formal institutional contexts.

# Research Question and Expectations

## Research Question

## Hypotheses

## Expectations

# Literature Review

# Methdology

## Definitions?

## Data Sources

## Data pipeline

### Data Collection

### Data Storage

### Data Cleaning

## Measures

### Linguistic Complexity Measures and WHY

### Measurement considerations

## Analysis Strategy

### Descriptive Analysis

### Time Trends

### Regression

## One-minute speeches extension

# Results

## Descriptive

## Model

# Discussion

## Interpretation of results

## Implications

# Limitations

## Data Limitations

## Measurement Limitations

## Conceptual Limitations

# Reproducibility

# Conclusion

# References
